mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Goldenseal contains isoquinoline alkaloids such as berberine and hydrastine; low berberine absorption from oral goldenseal may limit applicability of berberine-only studies. A controlled clinical study indicates goldenseal can precipitate drug interactions via inhibition pathways.
safetyNotes: Short-term doses around 3 g/day appeared tolerated in small studies; long-term safety is uncertain; products may be adulterated/mislabeled. Avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding and do not give to infants due to berberine risk.
description: A North American plant with bright yellow rhizome; native to northeastern U.S. and southeastern Canada; wild populations have been overharvested and are limited.
dosage: About 3 g/day has been used in small research studies for short periods.
interactions: Metformin levels decreased about 25% in a healthy-adult study with goldenseal extract plus metformin; broad interaction potential should be flagged.
preparation: Oral supplements commonly derived from rhizome/root; also used as mouthwash/eyewash and topically in some contexts, though human evidence is limited.
region: Native range is SE Canada to N. Central & E. U.S.A.
sideEffects: potential liver toxicity with long use
summary: Goldenseal is promoted for colds/URIs and other uses, but there is not enough evidence to determine usefulness and no rigorous human studies evaluating effects on health conditions. Short-term dosing around 3 g/day has appeared tolerated in small studies, but longer-term safety is uncertain.
